symbols.name: Config
symbols.detail: [USED] Returned by public parsing functions from_file and parse, used in tests | [UNIQUE] Struct definition with no similar types found in RAG results | [OK] Plain data struct; field types are correct and consistent with the rest of the module. | [LEAN] Two-field struct that maps directly to WireGuard config sections. No unnecessary indirection or generics. | [WEAK] The `parse` method is exercised by `parse_full_config` (happy path with two peers, port, address, keepalive) and `parse_v6_allowed_ips`. However, all error paths (key outside section, bad line without '=', no [Interface] section) are untested, and `from_file` has zero test coverage. | [PARTIAL] Has a struct-level `/// Parsed WireGuard configuration.` doc comment, but neither public field (`interface`, `peers`) carries a `///` comment explaining its role. No `# Examples` section for a public API type. (deliberated: confirmed — Tests WEAK is accurate: error paths (bad line, key outside section, no [Interface]) and from_file are untested. Documentation PARTIAL is fair: struct has a doc comment but the two public fields lack individual doc comments. For a two-field struct with self-descriptive names (interface, peers) missing field docs is minor, but PARTIAL is the correct classification.)

symbols.name: InterfaceConfig
symbols.detail: [USED] Returned by parse_interface, stored in Config.interface, essential data type | [UNIQUE] Struct definition with no similar types found in RAG results | [OK] Field types are appropriate. Storing the derived netmask as Ipv4Addr alongside the address is a design choice, not a bug. | [LEAN] Five fields that map one-to-one to WireGuard INI keys. Using Option<(Ipv6Addr, u8)> for optional dual-stack is appropriately minimal. | [GOOD] Pure data struct with no runtime behavior. Rule 6 applies: types/interfaces with no runtime logic are GOOD by default. Fields are indirectly populated and asserted in `parse_full_config`. | [UNDOCUMENTED] No `///` doc comment on the struct definition, and none of the five public fields (`private_key`, `listen_port`, `address`, `netmask`, `address_v6`) are documented. Completely absent for a public API type. (deliberated: confirmed — UNDOCUMENTED is correct: no doc comment at all on a public struct with five fields including non-obvious ones like netmask (stored separately from address) and address_v6 as Option<(Ipv6Addr, u8)>. A consumer needs to understand the tuple semantics. Tests are GOOD per rule 6 (pure data struct), no change needed there.)

symbols.name: PeerConfig
symbols.detail: [USED] Returned by parse_peer, stored in Config.peers vector, represents peer data | [UNIQUE] Struct definition with no similar types found in RAG results | [OK] All fields have correct types; Optional wrapping of endpoint, preshared_key, and keepalive is appropriate. | [LEAN] Five optional/required fields mirroring the WireGuard [Peer] spec precisely. No spurious abstraction. | [GOOD] Pure data struct with no runtime behavior. Rule 6 applies. Fields are exercised indirectly in `parse_full_config` (endpoint, allowed_ips, persistent_keepalive) and the optional-absent cases (endpoint=None, keepalive=None). | [UNDOCUMENTED] No `///` doc comment on the struct definition, and none of the five public fields (`public_key`, `preshared_key`, `endpoint`, `allowed_ips`, `persistent_keepalive`) carry any documentation. (deliberated: confirmed — UNDOCUMENTED is correct: public struct with five fields, no doc comments. Fields like allowed_ips (Vec<CidrAddr>) and the Option wrapping on several fields deserve explanation for API consumers.)

symbols.name: CidrAddr
symbols.detail: [USED] Contains IPv4/IPv6 CIDR logic via contains_v4, contains_v6, contains methods | [UNIQUE] Struct definition with no similar types found in RAG results | [OK] Struct fields addr: IpAddr and prefix_len: u8 are the correct types to represent a CIDR block for both v4 and v6. | [LEAN] Minimal two-field struct. The impl block exposes contains_v4/contains_v6 typed variants alongside the unified contains; the typed methods are genuinely useful when the caller already has a typed IP. The CIDR math is hand-rolled but no CIDR crate is listed as a dependency, so this is not a NIH violation. | [WEAK] `contains_v4` is well tested (normal /24, host /32, default /0, cross-family v4-vs-v6). `contains_v6` is only tested for /64 normal range; the /0 default-route and /128 host edge cases are missing. The public `contains` dispatcher method (which routes to contains_v4 or contains_v6) is never called directly in any test. | [PARTIAL] Has a meaningful struct-level doc comment (`/// An IP address with prefix length (CIDR notation). Supports v4 and v6.`), but neither public field (`addr`, `prefix_len`) has a `///` comment, and no `# Examples` section is present on this public type. (deliberated: confirmed — Tests WEAK is accurate: contains_v6 is missing /0 and /128 edge cases, and the public contains() dispatcher is never directly tested. Documentation PARTIAL is correct: struct has a meaningful doc comment but public fields lack individual docs and no examples are provided.)

symbols.name: decode_key
symbols.detail: [USED] Called in parse_interface (line 187) and parse_peer (lines 245, 247) for key decoding | [UNIQUE] Decodes base64 string to 32-byte key. RAG found base64_key (0.706-0.708 score) which is the inverse operation (encodes bytes to base64 string). Different parameters, return types, and semantics despite high similarity score indicate complementary functions, not duplicates. | [OK] BASE64_STANDARD requires padding; WireGuard 32-byte keys produce exactly 44-char base64 with one trailing '='. Because Config::parse uses split_once('=') on the assignment separator (the first '='), everything after it—including trailing base64 padding '='—is preserved in the value, so decoding is correct. | [LEAN] Eight-line helper: base64 decode then fixed-size array coercion. Both steps are needed; nothing extraneous. | [WEAK] Exercised indirectly on the happy path whenever `Config::parse` is called with valid base64 keys. Neither of the two error branches (invalid base64 encoding, decoded length != 32) is covered by any test. | [UNDOCUMENTED] Private function (`fn`, no `pub`) with no `///` doc comment. The name is reasonably self-descriptive, so lack of documentation is tolerable for a private helper, hence reduced confidence. (deliberated: confirmed — Tests WEAK confirmed: both error branches (invalid base64, wrong length) have no coverage. UNDOCUMENTED on a private function with a self-descriptive name warrants the already-reduced confidence of 60. The function name and signature make its purpose clear, so this is a very minor issue.)

symbols.name: parse_interface
symbols.detail: [USED] Helper called by Config::parse on line 215 to parse interface configuration section | [UNIQUE] Parses [Interface] config section. RAG found parse_peer (0.706 score) with similar structure (both iterate kvs, match keys, return Result<Config>). However, they parse different semantic domains: parse_interface extracts interface-specific fields (privatekey, listenport, address) returning InterfaceConfig, while parse_peer handles peer fields (publickey, endpoint, allowedips) returning PeerConfig. Different required fields, validation logic, and return types make them semantically distinct despite structural similarity. | [NEEDS_FIX] When an Address entry has no '/' prefix notation, the code unconditionally defaults to prefix '24' via unwrap_or((part, "24")). For an IPv6 address without an explicit prefix (e.g., 'Address = fd15::1'), prefix_len 24 is stored instead of the correct default of 128. By contrast, parse_cidr correctly branches on addr.is_ipv4() to pick 32 vs 128. In practice WireGuard configs always carry the CIDR prefix, but the inconsistency is a latent correctness bug. | [LEAN] Handles all mandatory and optional WireGuard Interface keys including dual-stack Address parsing. Length (~50 lines) is proportional to the number of real fields and their distinct parsing rules. The two-pass design (collect kvs then parse) is a clean scanning/validation separation, not an unnecessary layer. | [WEAK] `parse_full_config` checks listen_port, address, and netmask for the happy path. Missing coverage: dual-stack Address line (v4 + v6), missing PrivateKey error, missing Address error, malformed port, and the dual-stack v6 address field on the interface struct itself. Default ListenPort behavior is also never verified. | [UNDOCUMENTED] Private function with no `///` doc comment. The function has non-trivial behavior (default port of 51820, comma-separated address parsing, optional IPv6). A private item, so tolerable, but notable given its complexity. (deliberated: confirmed — Correction NEEDS_FIX is verified: line 196 uses unwrap_or((part, "24")) which assigns prefix 24 to IPv6 addresses missing a slash, while parse_cidr correctly branches on is_ipv4() to pick 32 vs 128. This is directly visible in the source code and is a genuine latent bug. Raising confidence to 85 since the inconsistency is unambiguous. Tests WEAK confirmed: missing coverage for dual-stack, missing PrivateKey, malformed port, default ListenPort. UNDOCUMENTED on a private function with non-trivial behavior (default port, comma-separated dual-stack parsing) is fair at reduced confidence.)

symbols.name: parse_peer
symbols.detail: [USED] Helper called by Config::parse on lines 130 and 137 for each peer configuration | [UNIQUE] Parses [Peer] config section. RAG found parse_interface (0.706 score) with similar structure. However, different fields parsed (publickey, presharedkey, endpoint, allowedips, persistentkeepalive vs privatekey, listenport, address), different return type (PeerConfig vs InterfaceConfig), and different helper calls (parse_cidr vs prefix_to_netmask) indicate distinct semantic contracts despite structural parallelism. | [OK] All fields are parsed and validated correctly. The standard-library SocketAddr parser handles both IPv4 and bracketed-IPv6 endpoint formats. AllowedIPs delegates correctly to parse_cidr. | [LEAN] Parallel structure to parse_interface, appropriate for the distinct Peer key set. No shared logic that could be unified without losing clarity; each key arm is simple. | [WEAK] `parse_full_config` covers endpoint, allowed_ips, and persistent_keepalive (present and absent). The `preshared_key` field is never exercised by any test. Error paths (missing PublicKey, malformed endpoint, malformed keepalive value) are not tested. | [UNDOCUMENTED] Private function with no `///` doc comment. Parses multiple peer fields with error handling; undocumented but private, so lower confidence applied per policy. (deliberated: confirmed — Tests WEAK confirmed: preshared_key is never exercised, error paths (missing PublicKey, malformed endpoint/keepalive) are untested. UNDOCUMENTED on a private function is fair at reduced confidence. The function has parallel structure to parse_interface and its behavior is reasonably inferable from context.)

symbols.name: parse_cidr
symbols.detail: [USED] Helper called by parse_peer on line 261 to parse CIDR addresses in allowed_ips | [UNIQUE] Parses CIDR notation string to IpAddr and prefix. No similar functions found in RAG results | [OK] Correctly infers the default prefix from the address family (32 for v4, 128 for v6) when no '/' is present. Parsing and error handling are sound. | [LEAN] Twelve-line function: split on '/', parse addr, pick protocol-appropriate default prefix. Concise and does exactly one job. | [WEAK] Exercised indirectly through `parse_full_config` (IPv4 CIDRs) and `parse_v6_allowed_ips` (IPv6 CIDR). The no-slash default-prefix path (bare IP address without '/') is never tested. Error paths (unparseable address, unparseable prefix) have no coverage. | [UNDOCUMENTED] Private function with no `///` doc comment. Contains notable implicit behavior (default prefix 32 for IPv4, 128 for IPv6 when omitted) that would benefit from documentation even internally. (deliberated: confirmed — Tests WEAK confirmed: the no-slash default-prefix path is never tested, nor are error paths (unparseable address/prefix). UNDOCUMENTED fair at reduced confidence for a private helper, though the inline comment documents the default-prefix behavior which partially mitigates.)

symbols.name: prefix_to_netmask
symbols.detail: [USED] Public function called in parse_interface on line 200 to convert CIDR prefix to netmask | [UNIQUE] Converts IPv4 prefix length to netmask representation. No similar functions found in RAG results | [OK] The three-way branch eliminates all overflow/underflow risk: prefix==0 returns 0.0.0.0 directly; prefix>=32 returns 255.255.255.255 directly; remaining 1-31 shift u32::MAX left by (32-prefix), which is always 1-31 bits—safe for u32. Ipv4Addr::from(u32) uses network (big-endian) byte order consistently with the arithmetic, producing correct netmasks (e.g., /24 → 0xFFFFFF00 → 255.255.255.0). | [LEAN] Nine-line direct bit-shift computation with correct edge-case guards for 0 and ≥32. Minimal and correct. | [WEAK] Only indirectly validated for prefix=24 via the `netmask` assertion in `parse_full_config`. The two explicit edge-case branches — prefix=0 (returns 0.0.0.0) and prefix>=32 (returns 255.255.255.255) — have no test coverage whatsoever. | [UNDOCUMENTED] Public function (`pub fn`) with no `///` doc comment at all. Converts a prefix length to an `Ipv4Addr` netmask with specific edge-case handling (prefix=0 → all zeros, prefix≥32 → all ones). Missing parameter description, return semantics, and `# Examples` for a public API. (deliberated: confirmed — Tests WEAK confirmed: only /24 is indirectly tested via parse_full_config; the explicit edge-case branches for prefix=0 and prefix>=32 have zero coverage. UNDOCUMENTED is a more significant finding here since this is a public function with specific edge-case behavior that callers need to understand. Confidence 85 is appropriate for a public API item.)

actions.description: Replace the hardcoded default prefix '24' with a type-aware default: parse the address first, then choose '32' for IPv4 and '128' for IPv6 when no prefix notation is present—mirroring the logic already used in parse_cidr.
Add JSDoc documentation for exported symbol: `InterfaceConfig`
Add JSDoc documentation for exported symbol: `PeerConfig`
Add JSDoc documentation for exported symbol: `prefix_to_netmask`

file_level.general_notes: Best practices score: 9/10 (0 FAIL, 2 WARN, 10 PASS)

rules.detail: No bare `.unwrap()` calls appear in production code paths. `unwrap_or` is used in `parse_interface` (L208) and `parse_cidr` (L279) which is safe. `.unwrap()` in the `#[cfg(test)]` module is exempt per rule 3 for test modules.
No `unsafe` blocks exist anywhere in this file.

All fallible operations use `?`, `map_err`, or `ok_or_else` with descriptive error messages. No silent ignores (empty `_ => {}` arms on match are intentional and correct for unknown config keys). Error context is preserved throughout.

rules.rule_name: Derive common traits on public types
rules.detail: `Config` and `InterfaceConfig` and `PeerConfig` only derive `Debug`, missing `Clone` and `PartialEq`. `CidrAddr` derives `Debug` and `Clone` but is missing `PartialEq`. As public config types used across crate boundaries, these traits are commonly expected for ergonomic usage (e.g., cloning a config for testing, comparing configs in integration tests).

Lifetime elision is correctly applied throughout. The local `&str` slices in `parse` (e.g., `iface_kvs: Vec<(&str, &str)>`) are visually explicit enough as lifetime is tied to the `input` parameter. No cases where explicit annotations would meaningfully improve clarity.

Code uses iterator patterns and idiomatic Rust constructs throughout. `to_ascii_lowercase().as_str()` for case-insensitive match is the accepted idiomatic pattern in Rust when match arms are needed. `unwrap_or` is used instead of manual `match` + default. No unnecessary clones detected; borrows are used where sufficient.
No `panic!` macro invocations in production code. Test module uses `.unwrap()` on `Config::parse` results, which is exempt per evaluation rules.

File is correctly placed in `rustguard-daemon/src/config.rs` consistent with the daemon sub-crate's role. Public surface (`Config`, `InterfaceConfig`, `PeerConfig`, `CidrAddr`, `prefix_to_netmask`) is appropriate. Private helpers (`decode_key`, `parse_interface`, `parse_peer`, `parse_cidr`) are correctly kept private. Minor note: `prefix_to_netmask` is `pub` but may only be needed internally; consider whether it belongs in the public API.

The module has a `//!` doc comment. `CidrAddr` and its methods are documented. However, `Config`, `InterfaceConfig`, `PeerConfig`, `Config::from_file`, `Config::parse`, and `prefix_to_netmask` all lack `///` doc comments. These are key public-facing types and functions that consumers of the crate need to understand.

The base64-encoded WireGuard private key `cGiPH7CqyNOCaW6ykZLH9K3Bt0enk5rDiTcv1O3A+JA=` and peer public keys appear only inside `#[cfg(test)]` as sample fixture data. These are test-only keys following the standard practice for parser unit tests and do not represent production credentials.

No `mem::forget`, `Box::into_raw`, or raw pointer manipulation. All types use standard ownership; `Vec`, `Option`, and fixed-size arrays are managed by the Rust allocator. No custom `Drop` implementation needed. No leak patterns detected.

All fields in `Config`, `InterfaceConfig`, `PeerConfig`, and `CidrAddr` are composed exclusively of `Send + Sync` standard-library types (`[u8; 32]`, `u16`, `Ipv4Addr`, `Ipv6Addr`, `IpAddr`, `SocketAddr`, `Vec`, `Option`). No shared mutable state, no interior mutability without synchronization, and no explicit threading constructs.

suggestions.description: Add missing derived traits to public config structs for ergonomic cross-crate usage
Add documentation comments to public API entry points
Add PartialEq to CidrAddr to enable equality comparisons without manual field checks
suggestions.after:
#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub struct CidrAddr {

deliberation.reasoning: Overall NEEDS_REFACTOR is the correct verdict. The file has one confirmed latent correctness bug (parse_interface defaulting IPv6 prefix to 24 instead of 128), multiple public types and a public function lacking documentation, and systematically weak test coverage on error paths. No actions need removal — the JSDoc terminology in actions 2-6 is a labeling artifact from the evaluator template but the intent (add Rust doc comments) is valid and the targets are correct. All axis findings are internally coherent: every flagged symbol is USED, so test and documentation improvements are worthwhile. No inter-axis conflicts detected. The best_practices score of 9/10 with two WARNs (missing derive traits and missing docs) aligns with the symbol-level findings.
